verses.text: After the death of Joshua it came to pass, that the children of Israel asked the LORD, saying, Who shall go up for us against the Canaanites first, to fight against them? The LORD said, Judah shall go up: behold, I have delivered the land into his hand. Judah said unto Simeon his brother, Come up with me into my lot, that we may fight against the Canaanites; and I likewise will go with thee into thy lot. So Simeon went with him. Guidance: The book opens well. Israel asks before acting; God answers; brothers cooperate. Judah and Simeon team up because their territories overlap (Simeon was embedded in Judah, Josh 19:1). This is exactly the kind of inquiry that should have happened before the Gibeonites in Joshua 9. The new generation begins where the old one began — asking the LORD.

Judah went up; and the LORD delivered the Canaanites and the Perizzites into their hand: they slew of them in Bezek ten thousand men. They found Adoni-bezek in Bezek: they fought against him, and they slew the Canaanites and the Perizzites. But Adoni-bezek fled; and they pursued after him, caught him, and cut off his thumbs and his great toes. Adoni-bezek said, Threescore and ten kings, having their thumbs and their great toes cut off, gathered their meat under my table: as I have done, so God hath requited me. They brought him to Jerusalem, and there he died. Guidance: Adoni-bezek had crippled seventy kings to keep them tame as palace pets; now he loses the same fingers and toes. He himself names the principle out loud: 'as I have done, so God hath requited me.' Pagan kings sometimes preach better sermons than the prophets — the law of harvest is universal. God's justice is not a punitive innovation in this verse; it is the moral physics that even Canaanites recognize.

Now the children of Judah had fought against Jerusalem, had taken it, and smitten it with the edge of the sword, and set the city on fire. Guidance: A brief note that Judah took Jerusalem and burned it. Yet verse 21 will show the Jebusites are still there — the city was not held. Conquest without occupation is loss with extra steps. The famous citadel of Zion will wait four more centuries for David to seize it (2 Sam 5). Half-finished work passes the bill to a future generation.

Afterward the children of Judah went down to fight against the Canaanites that dwelt in the mountain, in the south, and in the valley. Judah went against the Canaanites that dwelt in Hebron (now the name of Hebron before was Kirjath-arba): they slew Sheshai, Ahiman, Talmai. From thence he went against the inhabitants of Debir: the name of Debir before was Kirjath-sepher. Caleb said, He that smiteth Kirjath-sepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife. Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother, took it: and he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife. When she came to him, she moved him to ask of her father a field: she lighted from off her ass; Caleb said unto her, What wilt thou? She said unto him, Give me a blessing: for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. Caleb gave her the upper springs and the nether springs. Guidance: A flashback. The story of Othniel and Achsah was already told in Joshua 15 — repeated here because Othniel will be Israel's first judge (Judg 3:9-11). The book that opens with the wrong daughter (Achsah asking for springs) introduces the right son-in-law. Othniel earns his wife by faith and his place in scripture by future obedience. Watch how scripture lays down characters early.

The children of the Kenite, Moses' father in law, went up out of the city of palm trees with the children of Judah into the wilderness of Judah, which lieth in the south of Arad; they went and dwelt among the people. Judah went with Simeon his brother, and they slew the Canaanites that inhabited Zephath, and utterly destroyed it. And the name of the city was called Hormah. Guidance: The Kenites — Jethro's descendants — leave Jericho ('city of palm trees') and migrate south with Judah. Foreigners joining the chosen tribe and settling among them. The same family that gave Moses his wisest counsel (Exod 18) keeps doing kindness to Israel through the centuries. And Zephath becomes Hormah (Num 21:3) — a name meaning 'devotion to destruction.' The wilderness defeat is finally avenged generations later.

Judah took Gaza with the coast thereof, Askelon with the coast thereof, and Ekron with the coast thereof. The LORD was with Judah; and he drave out the inhabitants of the mountain; but could not drive out the inhabitants of the valley, because they had chariots of iron. Guidance: 'The LORD was with Judah; and he drave out the mountain… but could not drive out the valley.' The conjunction 'but' is the moral pivot. With the LORD there is no real 'could not'; what changes is the kind of terrain and the kind of resistance. Iron chariots are exactly what God promised through Joshua you could defeat (Josh 17:18). The chapter that began with God-given victories ends with reasons Israel could not finish. Reasons multiply faster than enemies when faith leaks.

They gave Hebron unto Caleb, as Moses said: and he expelled thence the three sons of Anak. The children of Benjamin did not drive out the Jebusites that inhabited Jerusalem; but the Jebusites dwell with the children of Benjamin in Jerusalem unto this day. Guidance: One verse Caleb the giant-slayer; the next verse Benjamin the giant-tolerater. Caleb drives out the same sons of Anak whose grandfather Adoni-bezek used to mutilate kings; Benjamin allows the Jebusites to keep living in Jerusalem (which Judah had burned, v8). Faith finishes the work; compromise leaves footprints. The phrase 'unto this day' was already noted in Joshua 15:63 — the Jebusite tenancy is a stubborn fact written into both books.

The house of Joseph also went up against Beth-el: and the LORD was with them. The house of Joseph sent to descry Beth-el. (Now the name of the city before was Luz.) The spies saw a man come forth out of the city, said unto him, Shew us, we pray thee, the entrance into the city, and we will shew thee mercy. When he shewed them the entrance into the city, they smote the city with the edge of the sword; but they let go the man and all his family. The man went into the land of the Hittites, built a city, and called the name thereof Luz: which is the name thereof unto this day. Guidance: The Rahab pattern repeats. An informant betrays the city in exchange for mercy. His extended family is spared. He then exits Israel's territory to found a new Luz in Hittite country — a foreign re-founding of his hometown's old name. The Hittite Luz on the map is a small footnote with a big lesson: God's mercy travels with the merciful, even when the merciful relocate. A Rahab story without the faith vocabulary.

Neither did Manasseh drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shean and her towns, nor Taanach and her towns, nor the inhabitants of Dor and her towns, nor the inhabitants of Ibleam and her towns, nor the inhabitants of Megiddo and her towns: but the Canaanites would dwell in that land. When Israel was strong, they put the Canaanites to tribute, and did not utterly drive them out. Neither did Ephraim drive out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer; but the Canaanites dwelt in Gezer among them. Guidance: A long catalogue of failures begins. Manasseh's list — Beth-shean, Taanach, Dor, Ibleam, Megiddo — names the strategic plain that will haunt Israel for centuries (Megiddo is where Josiah will die, 2 Kings 23:29; the very name behind 'Armageddon'). And the same pattern as Josh 17: 'when they were strong, they put them to tribute.' Translation: they could have driven them out. They chose income over obedience. Ephraim follows suit at Gezer. The chapter that started with 'who shall go up first' ends listing who never finished.

Neither did Zebulun drive out the inhabitants of Kitron, nor the inhabitants of Nahalol; but the Canaanites dwelt among them, and became tributaries. Neither did Asher drive out the inhabitants of Accho, nor the inhabitants of Zidon, nor of Ahlab, nor of Achzib, nor of Helbah, nor of Aphik, nor of Rehob: but the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: for they did not drive them out. Neither did Naphtali drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh, nor the inhabitants of Beth-anath; but he dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: nevertheless the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and of Beth-anath became tributaries unto them. Guidance: Read carefully — the phrasing of Asher reverses the pattern. With everyone else, the Canaanites dwell among Israel. With Asher, 'the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites.' The minority sits inside the majority. The lights of Israel are absorbed into the rooms of Canaan. The deeper the compromise, the closer it gets to the inversion of the original assignment. By the time you read 'tribute' three times in three verses, you can hear the chapter naming a slow disaster the people do not yet see.

Neither did Zebulun drive out the inhabitants of Kitron, nor the inhabitants of Nahalol; but the Canaanites dwelt among them, and became tributaries. Neither did Asher drive out the inhabitants of Accho, nor the inhabitants of Zidon, nor of Ahlab, nor of Achzib, nor of Helbah, nor of Aphik, nor of Rehob: but the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: for they did not drive them out. Neither did Naphtali drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh, nor the inhabitants of Beth-anath; but he dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: nevertheless the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and of Beth-anath became tributaries unto them. Guidance: Read carefully — the phrasing of Asher reverses the pattern. With everyone else, the Canaanites dwell among Israel. With Asher, 'the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites.' The minority sits inside the majority. The lights of Israel are absorbed into the rooms of Canaan. The deeper the compromise, the closer it gets to the inversion of the original assignment. By the time you read 'tribute' three times in three verses, you can hear the chapter naming a slow disaster the people do not yet see.

Neither did Zebulun drive out the inhabitants of Kitron, nor the inhabitants of Nahalol; but the Canaanites dwelt among them, and became tributaries. Neither did Asher drive out the inhabitants of Accho, nor the inhabitants of Zidon, nor of Ahlab, nor of Achzib, nor of Helbah, nor of Aphik, nor of Rehob: but the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: for they did not drive them out. Neither did Naphtali drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh, nor the inhabitants of Beth-anath; but he dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: nevertheless the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and of Beth-anath became tributaries unto them. Guidance: Read carefully — the phrasing of Asher reverses the pattern. With everyone else, the Canaanites dwell among Israel. With Asher, 'the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites.' The minority sits inside the majority. The lights of Israel are absorbed into the rooms of Canaan. The deeper the compromise, the closer it gets to the inversion of the original assignment. By the time you read 'tribute' three times in three verses, you can hear the chapter naming a slow disaster the people do not yet see.

The Amorites forced the children of Dan into the mountain: for they would not suffer them to come down to the valley: but the Amorites would dwell in mount Heres in Aijalon, and in Shaalbim: yet the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed, so that they became tributaries. The coast of the Amorites was from the going up to Akrabbim, from the rock, and upward. Guidance: Now the reversal is complete. Dan is not failing to drive out Canaanites; Canaanites are driving out Dan. The chapter ends with Israel's smallest western tribe pushed up into the hills, unable to descend. By Judges 18 they will give up and migrate to the far north. The book that opened with 'who shall go up first' against the Canaanites ends with one tribe forced upward by the Canaanites. The wheel of judges is already turning, and the next chapter will name what God thinks of all this.
